items.q: Does light always travel at the same speed?
items.correct: Only in a vacuum; in any material medium (water, glass) light travels more slowly, which is what causes refraction.
items.misconception: Yes — c is a universal constant in every situation.
items.random_wrong: No — light always slows with distance from its source.
items.why: Students take 'c is constant' as 'c everywhere' and miss the qualifier 'in a vacuum'.

items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: What property of a sound wave determines its pitch?
items.correct: The frequency — higher frequency means higher pitch.
items.misconception: Amplitude determines pitch — a bigger wave is a higher-pitched note.
items.random_wrong: The speed of the wave determines pitch — faster waves sound higher.
items.why: Students conflate the two main wave properties: amplitude relates to volume, frequency to pitch, but they often swap them.

items.q: A car travels 4 km north then 3 km east. What is its displacement magnitude?
items.why: Correct answer: 5 km. “7 km” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
items.why: It's Kinetic energy. “Acceleration” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
